current_books.title: Parable of the Sower
current_books.author: Octavia E. Butler
current_books.why: Walled communities, climate collapse, a teenage prophet on the road. The bone-deep dread of The Road meets the political thrust of Bacigalupi — and it was written in 1993.

current_books.title: The Ministry for the Future
current_books.author: Kim Stanley Robinson
current_books.why: A polyphonic climate novel that opens with a heatwave scene as devastating as anything in The Water Knife, then pivots into oddly hopeful policy wonkery.

current_books.title: American War
current_books.author: Omar El Akkad
current_books.why: A second US civil war in the 2070s, fought over the last fossil fuels. Spare, devastating prose — distinctly McCarthy-coded, with a slow-burn protagonist arc.

current_books.title: Gold Fame Citrus
current_books.author: Claire Vaye Watkins
current_books.why: California's water is gone and a dune sea is swallowing the Southwest. Lyrical and hallucinatory — basically a literary sibling to The Water Knife.

current_books.title: Neuromancer
current_books.author: William Gibson
current_books.why: You already love Gibson's Sprawl Trilogy, and this is the novel that launched it — the dense, hallucinatory ur-text of cyberpunk that Snow Crash was consciously riffing on. If it somehow slipped past you, it's essential.

current_books.title: Hardwired
current_books.author: Walter Jon Williams
current_books.why: Published in 1986 and often overlooked beside Gibson, it shares Altered Carbon's bruising noir physicality and corporate-dystopia grit — mercenaries, black-market implants, and a scorched post-war America run by orbital cartels.

current_books.title: The Quantum Thief
current_books.author: Hannu Rajaniemi
current_books.why: A post-cyberpunk caper across a transhuman solar system. Dense, dazzling, throws you in the deep end exactly the way Neuromancer did.

current_books.title: Blindsight
current_books.author: Peter Watts
current_books.why: First-contact horror with transhumans and predator-vampires interrogating consciousness itself. Brutally smart — Hyperion fans tend to love it too.

current_books.title: A Memory Called Empire
current_books.author: Arkady Martine
current_books.why: A small-station ambassador navigates the seductive horror of a vast empire. Reads like Hyperion crossed with Le Guin's anthropological eye. The sequel is just as good.

current_books.title: The Player of Games
current_books.author: Iain M. Banks
current_books.why: The friendliest doorway into the Culture. Scope, civilisational politics, and moral weight that should hit your Dune and Hyperion nerve. Banks is the missing author from your list.

current_books.title: A Fire Upon the Deep
current_books.author: Vernor Vinge
current_books.why: Like Children of Time, it builds radically alien minds with genuine rigour — pack-brained Tines whose cognition emerges from the group — while delivering the epic, idea-dense scope of Hyperion and Three-Body Problem.

current_books.title: House of Suns
current_books.author: Alastair Reynolds
current_books.why: A six-million-year galactic timescale, with clones of a single woman trading memories at meet-ups every 200,000 years. Hyperion-grade scope and melancholy.

current_books.title: The Blade Itself
current_books.author: Joe Abercrombie
current_books.why: You already love Abercrombie, so if The First Law trilogy itself has somehow not been read front-to-back, this is the starting point — it broadens from Malazan's vast military canvas into something more character-intimate and bleakly funny.

current_books.title: The Black Company
current_books.author: Glen Cook
current_books.why: Grimdark before grimdark was a word. Mercenary-company POV, blood-and-mud morality — the obvious ancestor of Abercrombie's First Law.

current_books.title: Assassin's Apprentice
current_books.author: Robin Hobb
current_books.why: Slow-burn, ferocious character work across a 16-book braided saga. The author Rothfuss readers most often graduate to — and she actually finishes her series.

current_books.title: The Poppy War
current_books.author: R. F. Kuang
current_books.why: Military academy to genocide-scale war, drawn from 20th-century Chinese history. Brutal, modern, GRRM-territory stakes — not for the faint-hearted.

current_books.title: Imperium
current_books.author: Robert Harris
current_books.why: You already love Harris, and his Cicero trilogy is his finest historical work — a forensically researched, politically thrilling account of Roman power that shares Wolf Hall's interest in men who survive by intelligence rather than force.

current_books.title: Shōgun
current_books.author: James Clavell
current_books.why: An Englishman shipwrecked into Sengoku-era Japan. Massive, addictive, written with the same propulsive instinct as Cornwell's Saxon books.

current_books.title: Master and Commander
current_books.author: Patrick O'Brian
current_books.why: Napoleonic naval fiction across 20 books. The more literary cousin of Cornwell's Sharpe — quieter, funnier, profoundly companionable once you tune in to the rhythm.

current_books.title: HHhH
current_books.author: Laurent Binet
current_books.why: The 1942 plot to assassinate Reinhard Heydrich, told as a meta-narrative about the difficulty of telling it. Slim, riveting — Robert Harris would adore this one.

current_books.title: The Raw Shark Texts
current_books.author: Steven Hall
current_books.why: It shares Piranesi's quality of treating an impossible, self-enclosed logic as completely earnest — a man hunted by a conceptual shark through fractured memory and text — while matching David Mitchell's playful structural ambition.

current_books.title: Never Let Me Go
current_books.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
current_books.why: Quiet dystopia disguised as a boarding-school memoir. Devastating in the same way Cloud Atlas is devastating, but more concentrated.

current_books.title: Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell
current_books.author: Susanna Clarke
current_books.why: Two magicians revive English magic during the Napoleonic Wars. Footnoted, funny, immense — the texture of Le Guin with the wit of an Austen novel.

current_books.title: The Memory Police
current_books.author: Yoko Ogawa
current_books.why: On a small island, things vanish from memory one by one — birds, ribbons, novels, body parts. Ishiguro-quiet, Atwood-political, hauntingly beautiful.
